title: Defect Detection in Manufacturing
concept: Manufacturing defect detection deploys anomaly detection on production lines to catch cracks, scratches, misalignments, and contamination in real time, balancing detection sensitivity against false alarm rates.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why might a normal-but-unfamiliar variation (like a new but valid cap color) trigger a false positive in a reconstruction-based detector?

options.text: The autoencoder never learned that specific normal variation well, producing higher reconstruction error for it
New colors always break the camera hardware
False positives cannot occur in reconstruction-based detection

exercises.explain: Yes! Unfamiliar-but-valid inputs can still trigger elevated reconstruction error.

cards.title: Where anomaly detection earns its keep
cards.body: Manufacturing lines produce thousands of identical parts per hour. A single missed crack or contamination event can mean a costly recall — this is exactly the high-stakes, high-volume environment where visual anomaly detection shines.

cards.title: Manufacturing Defect Detection
cards.body: In production settings, anomaly detection systems run cameras over parts moving down a conveyor belt, flagging cracks, scratches, dents, misalignments, discoloration, or contamination in real time — all using models trained mostly or entirely on images of correctly manufactured 'good' parts.

cards.title: A tireless inspector who never blinks
cards.body: A human quality inspector gets tired after hours of staring at identical parts and can miss subtle defects due to fatigue. An automated anomaly detection camera never blinks, never gets bored, and applies the exact same standard to the 10,000th part as the 1st.

cards.title: Battery cell inspection
cards.body: Battery manufacturers use anomaly detection to spot micro-cracks or seal misalignments in battery cells at extremely high inspection speeds — critical because a defective cell can pose a fire risk, and manual inspection can't keep pace with production line speed.

cards.title: Sensitivity vs. false alarms is a real tradeoff
cards.body: It's tempting to think you should crank sensitivity to catch every possible defect. But an overly sensitive detector flags too many normal parts as false positives, overwhelming human reviewers and slowing the line — engineers must tune the sensitivity threshold to balance catching real defects against manageable false alarm rates (callback to precision/recall from Unit 128).

left.text:
Misses real defects (false negatives)
Fewer alerts overall
Risk: defective products ship out

right.text:
Flags many normal parts (false positives)
Overwhelms human reviewers
Risk: line slowdowns, wasted good parts

exercises.prompt: What is a key advantage of automated anomaly detection over human visual inspection on a fast production line?

options.text: It applies a consistent standard without fatigue, unlike a human inspector who can tire over long shifts
It requires zero training data of any kind
It never produces any false alarms
It automatically fixes any defects it finds

exercises.explain: Correct! Consistency without fatigue is a major advantage of automated visual inspection.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Setting an anomaly detection threshold as sensitive as possible is always the best strategy for manufacturing inspection.
exercises.explain: False. Overly sensitive thresholds create excessive false positives, overwhelming reviewers and slowing production.

exercises.sentence: Engineers must balance catching real defects against a manageable false ___ rate.
exercises.explain: Balancing detection sensitivity against false alarms is a core manufacturing deployment tradeoff.

exercises.prompt: Why is battery cell inspection a particularly high-stakes application of manufacturing anomaly detection?

options.text: A defective cell can pose a fire safety risk, and manual inspection can't keep pace with production speed
Battery cells cannot be photographed by cameras
There is no safety risk associated with defective battery cells

exercises.explain: Correct! Safety risk plus production speed makes this a strong fit for automated anomaly detection.

exercises.prompt: A factory sets its anomaly detection threshold extremely tight to 'never miss a single defect.' Production line supervisors soon complain the system is flagging far too many perfectly good parts, causing major slowdowns. What is happening?

options.text: The overly tight threshold is producing many false positives, since even minor normal variations now get flagged as anomalous
The system has stopped working entirely and is producing random output
This means every single part on the line is now actually defective
Tighter thresholds always reduce false positives, so this cannot be the cause

exercises.explain: Exactly! Overly tight thresholds trade fewer misses for more false alarms — a real deployment tradeoff. Next: the unique challenges of evaluating anomaly detection systems fairly.
